item.description: Inquiry will look at whether Australia will receive the submarines and whether it will make the country safer.

item.title: Statement of intent to strike Beirut caused Hezbollah leaders to flee, disrupted surprise attack
item.description: The element of surprise was lost when Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu and Defense Minister Israel Katz announced the IDF's intentions to strike targets in the Lebanese capital. Multiple Hezbollah leaders fled Beirut after a joint statement from Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu and Defense Minister Israel Katz caused any IDF strikes in the capital to lose the element of surprise, an IDF source said on Monday.

item.title: 11-Year-Old Boy Disappeared in River as Police Say Family Is Living 'Every Parent's Worst Nightmare’
item.description: Local police identified the missing person as Mackenzie Swift on Monday, June 1

item.title: Germany's long-distance rail punctuality falls to 61.3% in May
item.description: The punctuality of Germany's long-distance trains remained at a low level in May, with 61. 3% of ICE and Intercity services arriving on time, according to internal data from state-owned rail operator Deutsche Bahn.

item.title: Fact Check: REUSED Video of Masked People With Torches Does NOT Show Church In UK's Yorkshire
item.description: Does a viral video actually show a group of people attempting to set a Yorkshire church on fire in broad daylight? No, that's not true: The clip showed a place of worship in Mexico, not the U.K. Images of the...

item.title: 'Retailer recycling lottery' leaves millions of banned vapes tossed each week as battery fires rise
item.description: "Vapes are one of the most environmentally wasteful, damaging, and dangerous consumer products ever sold."

item.title: Euphrates flood deprives east Syria farmers from crops
item.description: Farmer Issa al-Moussa walks among his damaged wheat crop in eastern Syria after the nearby Euphrates River flooded in recent days due to heavy rainfall and increased flows from Turkey.Turkish media however quoted official sources as saying that water authorities carried out "controlled water releases" from the Ataturk Dam after water levels rose due to heavy rainfall in recent months.

item.title: Jared Kushner's overseas luxury resort project faces anti-corruption investigation amid violent protests
item.description: Albania's SPAK opens investigation into luxury resort project linked to Jared Kushner as violent protests over the coastal development intensify.

item.title: ‘Don’t Let Me Die’: 11-Year-Old Loses Leg in Brazil Shark Attack (Video)
item.description: The incident occurred at a Brazilian beach known for a history of shark encounters. Doctors were forced to amputate his leg following the incident at Piedade Beach.

item.title: Vital to keep a UN force in Lebanon after current peacekeepers depart: Guterres
item.description: UN chief Antonio Guterres said Monday that peacekeepers will be needed in Lebanon after the mandate of the current mission expires at year-end -- an option likely to face opposition from the United States and Israel.Last August, the UN Security Council, under US pressure, decided to end the mandate of the United Nations Interim Force in Lebanon (UNIFIL) on December 31, 2026.

item.title: Australia says wheat crop set to plunge
item.description: Australia's wheat crop is set to fall by more than a quarter this season, a government report said Tuesday, as farmers face dry conditions and a surge in fuel and fertiliser prices fed by conflict in the Middle East.Farmers were expected to cut the total area planted with wheat by 12 percent, it said, citing lower profitability compared to other crops and "very dry" conditions in northern regions.

item.title: Powerful magnitude 6.2 earthquake rattles parts of Southern Italy
item.description: Residents in southern Italy were rattled by a magnitude 6.2 earthquake during the early hours of Tuesday morning. The U.S. Geological Survey reported that the earthquake struck about 10 miles southwest of Saracena, Italy, at 6:12 p.m. EST

item.title: Powerful earthquake strikes off Italy's southern coast
item.description: A powerful earthquake struck off the coast of Italy's Calabria region early on Tuesday and was felt as far away as Naples, news agency ANSA reported. The earthquake's epicentre was in the Tyrrhenian Sea off the city of Cosenza, some 240 kilometres south-east of Naples.

item.title: Canada is failing the Jewish community and Jews are being targeted, Prime Minister Carney says
item.description: Canada is failing Jewish Canadians and the community is being brutally targeted by hate, Prime Minister Mark Carney said Monday. Carney said across Canada, antisemitism has surged to levels not seen in the post-World War II era.

item.description: The secretary of State called the visit deeply meaningful.

item.description: There are currently no approved drugs that target Bundibugyo - the species of Ebola responsible for this outbreak.

item.description: Hundreds of residents in central Kenya marched Monday in protest of a proposed U.S.-run field hospital for U.S. citizens exposed to Ebola.

item.title: Iraqi national pleads not guilty in 18 attacks in Europe, calling himself a 'prisoner of war'
item.description: An Iraqi national accused of plotting at least 18 attacks in Europe in retaliation for the U.S. and Israel’s war with Iran pleaded not guilty on Monday before calling himself a “prisoner of war” and telling a judge that children and women were being killed “by your rockets.” Mohammad Baqer Saad Dawood Al-Saadi finally was persuaded to sit down in Manhattan federal court as two marshals approached him at a judge's urging. Al-Saadi did not appear to be trying to be disruptive as he commented beyond his response to the charges.

item.title: Peruvian shamans perform a blessing ritual ahead of a presidential runoff
item.description: Peruvian shamans gathered on Monday for a blessing ritual for the two candidates in the country’s presidential runoff next weekend, a decisive vote for the South American country that has seen a revolving door of presidents kicked out of office over corruption scandals. The two — Keiko Fujimori, the conservative daughter of disgraced former President Alberto Fujimori, and Roberto Sánchez, a nationalist congressman and former minister — are neck and neck in the polls and will face off on Sunday.

item.title: Monday’s Mini-Report, 6.1.26

item.title: Mexican police tear-gas teachers' protest 10 days before World Cup
item.description: Mexico City police hurled tear gas at protesting teachers to keep them from reaching the historic square where the "Fan fest" for the 2026 World Cup is under construction, according to AFP journalists.The clash started when teachers broke through one of the metal barriers that have been set up at the entrance to the Zocalo plaza, a block from the government palace and a giant screen where fans will watch Mexico's first World Cup game on June 11.

item.title: British woman falls 1,600ft to death while hiking in Spain
item.description: A British woman has died after falling while hiking in northern Spain.

item.title: Entrepreneur pilot leaves Paraguay before $3.6M in hashish found
item.description: Paraguayan authorities are probinh a suspected international drug-trafficking operation after discovering 577 pounds of hashish on a private jet.

item.title: 'We Shall Rise' installation highlights Israel's journey since October 7 at Tel Aviv mall
item.description: “We wanted to show that no matter what, no matter how they try to kill us and to make us vanish, we're still here,” writer Noam Horev explained. We Shall Rise, a new immersive art installation designed to take participants into Israeli society’s defining moments of, and since, the October 7, 2023, Hamas-led massacres, is now set to open to the public.

item.title: WHO chief wraps up visit to Ebola-hit Congo, briefs president on response

item.title: Trump speaks to Netanyahu as Hezbollah claims to be ready for full ceasefire
item.description: The speaker of the Lebanese parliament, Nabih Berri, informed US President Donald Trump's administration of Hezbollah's willingness to cooperate, Axios reported, citing Berri's top advisor. US President Donald Trump and Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu spoke on Monday, with the assumption being that they were discussing the potential for a ceasefire between Israel and Hezbollah, as communicated to the United States by Lebanon.

item.title: Protesters call on Kenyan government to halt femicide crisis

item.title: Lebanon says Hezbollah accepts US plan to stop attacks with Israel
item.description: The halt to Israeli attacks on Hezbollah announced earlier by US President Donald Trump applies to the southern suburbs of Beirut, Lebanese President Joseph Aoun said. "Under the proposed arrangement, Israeli strikes on Beirut's southern suburbs would cease in exchange for Hezbollah refraining from carrying out attacks against Israel," the Lebanese presidency said in a statement posted on X late Monday.

item.title: European Union reaches deal to fast-track illegal migrant returns

(Reporting by ​Gnaneshwar ‌Rajan in BengaluruEditing ​by Nick ​Zieminski).

item.title: IDF 146th Division completes southern Lebanon operations, as attacks on Hezbollah intensify
item.description: The IDF also announced on Sunday morning that its ground troops had taken over the Beaufort Ridge outpost and Wadi al-Saluki areas beyond the Litani River The IDF's 146th Division completed its activities in southern Lebanon after three months and would be brought back into Israel, the military announced on Monday. item.title: Energy crisis disrupts Cuba's education system
item.description: Cuba's ongoing energy crisis is taking a heavy toll on the country's education system, with lack of resources, teachers and transportation.

item.title: Latvia calls on Ukraine's drone experts after airspace scares
item.description: Ukrainian drone warfare specialists are set to travel to Latvia in the coming weeks to advise the Baltic country on countering unmanned aerial threats, Latvian Prime Minister Andris Kulbergs said on Monday. The experts will share their experience and help develop a long-term plan for building a layered air defence system capable of responding to different types of threats, Kulbergs said after meeting Ukrainian Prime Minister Yulia Svyrydenko in Riga.

item.title: Canada's Next-Gen Spy Planes Won't Be Coming From The USA

item.title: Trump says Israel, Hezbollah to halt attacks
item.description: US President Donald Trump said on Monday that Israel and the Iran-backed Hezbollah movement in Lebanon had agreed to halt their attacks on each other for the time being. Trump also said Israel would not send troops into the Lebanese capital Beirut, despite earlier threats to do so.

Earlier Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu announced a decision to strike Beirut's southern suburbs, widely known as Dahiyeh, prompting anxiety among residents and disruptions to daily life.

item.description: The cruise ship at the centre of a hantavirus outbreak has been disinfected and is ready to set sail again with passengers, its operator said on Monday. The Hondius is to depart on June 6 for Norwegian Arctic archipelago of Svalbard.

item.title: A secret bunker, tunnel and a Star of David tell a story of Jewish resistance in a Polish town
item.description: A secret bunker, an underground tunnel and an armband bearing the Star of David are among the rare findings in a house in southern Poland that was used by Jews, including young members of the resistance, to hide from the Nazis. “This armband is a witness, it’s like directly touching that evil which people created for other people,” Karolina Jakoweńko from the Cukerman’s Gate Foundation, which organized the search, told The Associated Press.

item.title: Confirmed Ebola cases in Congo reach 282 as survivors describe their recoveries
item.description: At least 282 confirmed cases of Ebola have now been reported in Congo’s ongoing outbreak, the central African nation said late Sunday, as patients who recovered from the disease spoke of their indescribable joy in interviews with The Associated Press. The outbreak remains focused in Congo's eastern Ituri province, where 264 of the cases have been recorded, Congo’s Ministry of Health said.

item.title: New executions in Iran following protests
item.description: Two more men have been executed in Iran in connection with the January uprising, the judiciary-linked Mizan news agency reported on Monday. Mehdad Mohammadi-Nia and Ashkan Maleki were accused of acts including setting fire to a mosque during the mass protests.

item.title: Life in prison for Syrian convicted of Islamist stabbing in Germany
item.description: A Syrian man convicted of an Islamist terrorist stabbing in the west German city of Bielefeld last year was sentenced on Monday to life in prison and will remain in preventive detention if his sentence ends early due to the seriousness of his crimes, the court announced. The ruling echos the demands of federal prosecutors.

item.title: Eight killed in further clashes between Hezbollah and Israel in southern Lebanon
item.description: The conflict between Israel and the Iran-backed Lebanese Hezbollah militia escalated further, with eight people killed and 19 injured in an Israeli overnight attack in southern Lebanon, Lebanon's state news agency NNA reported on Monday. Hezbollah, a Shiite militia, has meanwhile stepped up its attacks on northern Israel.

item.title: China issues new rules to bust 'ghost' takeout deliveries
item.description: Food sellers on Chinese delivery platforms must have physical shopfronts and indicate if they do not offer dine-in services under new rules unveiled on Monday to stamp out "ghost" takeout merchants.Merchants on food delivery platforms must now have physical shopfronts and label themselves if they do not offer dine-in services, according to the SAMR's new rules.

item.description: Abelardo de la Espriella, who has painted himself as a Trump supporter, will face left-wing Iván Cepeda in the runoff elections.

item.title: Philippine senator charged with plunder says he will surrender
item.description: A Philippine senator said he will surrender to authorities after a special anti-graft court ordered his arrest on Monday on a non-bailable charge of plunder after he allegedly pocketed a huge kickback in a flood-control project in the latest crisis to hit the upper chamber. The special Sandiganbayan anti-graft court had initially issued a warrant for Sen. Jinggoy Estrada’s arrest Friday on a graft charge that was bailable. Estrada, 63, has strongly denied allegations mainly by a former government public works engineer that he received more than 570 million pesos ($9.3 million) in kickbacks from flood control projects.

item.title: Many injured Ukrainians in latest Russian attacks
item.description: About a dozen people have been injured in Russia's latest attacks on Ukraine, officials in the war-torn country said on Monday. In the border city of Kharkiv in north-eastern Ukraine, two people were injured and two apartment blocks were hit, Governor Oleh Syniehubov wrote on Telegram.

item.title: Vape-related fires surge 450pc as batteries explode
item.description: Vape-related fires across Britain have soared by more than 450pc as lithium batteries explode, a major insurance firm has warned.

item.title: Australia economy minister says 'legitimate' fears driving rise of far-right
item.description: Australia's economy minister said Monday that "legitimate" concerns were driving a rise in support for the far right after a bombshell opinion poll showed the populist One Nation is now the country's most popular party."I think people have legitimate concerns about where they fit in the economy," Treasurer Jim Chalmers said when asked what was driving that.

item.title: US strikes Iranian targets along Strait of Hormuz as Kuwait defends against drones, missiles
item.description: According to CENTCOM, the attacks were in response to Iran's downing of an American MQ-1 Reaper drone. The United States struck Iranian targets in Goruk and Qeshm Island, both located along the Strait of Hormuz, US Central Command (CENTCOM) announced on Monday on X/Twitter.

item.title: German court to rule on terrorist attack on football fans in Bielefeld
item.description: A German court is due to deliver its verdict on Monday in the trial of a Syrian man who has confessed to carrying out an Islamist stabbing attack that seriously wounded four people outside a bar in the western city of Bielefeld last year. Federal prosecutors have asked the Higher Regional Court in Düsseldorf to sentence the 36-year-old defendant, identified as Mahmoud M under German privacy rules, to life imprisonment for four counts of attempted murder.

item.title: Video showing sea of hajj pilgrims at Mount Arafat is AI-generated
item.description: Huge numbers of Muslims defied searing desert temperatures to pray on Mount Arafat at the climax of the hajj pilgrimage, but video purportedly showing a sea of worshippers at the site is AI-generated. The video is inconsistent with footage filmed by news organisations including AFP, and Mount Arafat's landscape, and was also flagged as synthetic by AI detection software.

item.title: Litterbugs now face on-the-spot fines in Tokyo's tourist hotspot
item.description: Dozens of officials will patrol world-famous Shibuya as Japan responds to the impacts of overtourism.

item.title: Rescuers find new access route in search for two missing Laos miners
item.description: Rescuers searching for two miners missing in a flooded cave system in Laos said they had discovered a previously unknown entrance that could provide access to the area where the men are believed to be trapped. Australian cave diver Josh Richards told dpa that he, French cave expert Robin Cuesta and soldiers found the opening after trekking through dense forest in the mountainous Xaisomboun province.

item.title: France's Macron says French Navy boarded Russia-linked oil tanker
item.description: PARIS, June 1 () - The French Navy on Sunday boarded an oil tanker, named the Tagor, which was subject ‌to international sanctions and sailing from Russia, French President Emmanuel ‌Macron wrote on X. "This operation took place in the Atlantic Ocean, on the high ​seas, with the support of several partners, including the United Kingdom, in strict compliance with the law of the sea," he said.

item.title: Returning to the fold? Some young Spaniards embrace Catholicism and can’t wait for Pope Leo’s visit
item.description: Until three years ago, Sara Cabral’s faith experience was on trend with other Southern European youth — a “Catholic but never practicing” upbringing with little relevance to her life on Spain’s Canary Islands. “You get a restlessness about an emptiness that you don’t know how to fill,” Cabral, 26, says of her embrace of Catholicism. On trips to Spain this month and France in September, Leo will find thousands of young people like her in these traditionally Catholic but now staunchly secular countries, where historic churches are abundant and Mass attendance is sparse.

item.title: Ukraine pairs MBDA Meteor missiles with Saab Gripen fighter to counter Russian glide bombs
item.description: Ukraine's air defense gap against Russian glide bombs may have an answer in the Gripen paired with the Meteor beyond-visual-range missile.

item.title: London’s deepening childlessness crisis
item.description: Having children in inner London is becoming rarer. But Jonny Singer, a 34-year-old dad in Islington who works in sports administration, is pleased that he and his wife have bucked the trend.

item.title: Iran attacks damage 20 US military sites since start of war, satellite images show
item.description: Analysts told BBC Verify that Tehran's strikes were more extensive than publicly acknowledged.

item.title: Felled tree transformed into prayer sculpture
item.description: The 175-year-old cedar had to be cut down after another tree fell during storms last year.

item.title: Brisbane begins work on the 2032 Olympics main stadium at Victoria Park after protesters moved
item.description: Protestors have been moved along and excavation machinery has been moved in as work on the main stadium for the 2032 Olympics got under way Monday, almost five years after Brisbane was awarded rights to the Summer Games. The Games Independent Infrastructure and Coordination Authority took ownership of the inner city Victoria Park site from the Brisbane City Council at midnight, and temporary perimeter fencing was erected ahead of construction on the 3.6 billion Australian dollar ($2.6 billion) Brisbane Olympics main stadium.

item.title: Polls open in Ethiopia's election that is widely expected to be won by the ruling party
item.description: Polls opened Monday in Ethiopia in an election that is widely expected to be won by the ruling party. A heavy military presence was observed in the capital, Addis Ababa, as observers called for a peaceful election in the country that is Africa's second-most populous and hosts the headquarters of the African Union. Ethiopians are choosing more than 500 members of the House of Representatives, who will subsequently vote to select the prime minister.

item.title: Police Commissioner meets with members of LGBTQ+ community ahead of Pride Month
item.description: Police and LGBTQ+ community leaders discussed security preparations as Pride Month events get underway across Israel, with the Tel Aviv Pride Parade returning to its traditional format. Police Commissioner Danny Levy met with representatives of Israel's LGBTQ+ community on Sunday, reaffirming the Israel Police's commitment to ensuring that Pride Month events throughout June are held safely and securely.

item.title: Malaysia enforces ban on social media accounts for children younger than 16
item.description: Malaysia on Monday began enforcing rules barring millions of children younger than 16 from owning social media accounts, joining a global effort to tighten online safety protections for young users. The rules require social media platforms to implement age-verification systems and block users under 16 from creating accounts. The government said the measures are aimed at protecting children from harmful content, cyberbullying and platform features designed to encourage excessive use.

item.title: Ex-Prince Andrew Under Investigation For Alleged Encounter With Woman At Royal Ascot
item.description: Former prince Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor is back in the crosshairs of police in England ... he's under investigation for an alleged encounter back in the day with a woman at Royal Ascot. Cops across the pond are reportedly looking into an alleged incident from 2002 at the famous racing festival ... with police "examining events in 2002 as they consider wider possible crimes by Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor, including sexual misconduct, ­corruption and fraud" ... according to The Sunday Times. Royal Ascot is the most prestigious horse racing event in England with a rich history of tradition dating back centuries ... and in 2002, it was reportedly attended by Andrew's mom, Queen Elizabeth, his older brother the future King Charles, his younger brother Prince Edward, and his older daughter Princess Beatrice.

item.description: French President Emmanuel Macron has condemned the riots that broke out nationwide following Paris Saint-Germain's Champions League victory over Arsenal, vowing a tough response. "That's not soccer, that's not sports, that's not what we love," Macron said while receiving the French side at the Élysée Palace following their return from Budapest, according to French broadcaster BFMTV.

item.title: Indigenous leader Brooklyn Rivera dies in Nicaragua after nearly 3 years of detention
item.description: Brooklyn Rivera, a renowned Indigenous leader from Nicaragua who spent years fighting for the rights of his community and was imprisoned by the government in September 2023, has died. The Nicaraguan government issued a statement Sunday saying that Rivera died from a bacterial infection after his health had declined following a case of COVID-19, which led to his physical and neurological deterioration. Human rights activists and groups worldwide denounced his death and a previous statement by the government in which they referred to Rivera as “Brother” and said they were praying for him.

item.description: The soldier who killed the terrorist also provided emergency medical treatment to one of the wounded, saving their life, a spokesperson from the Gush Etzion Regional Council said. In an interview with US broadcaster CBS, Zelensky said the Russian army has been increasingly on the back foot since the end of December, suffering heavy losses.

item.title: Rescuers say a blast at a building storing explosives in Myanmar has killed more than 45 people
item.description: A blast on Sunday at a building in northeastern Myanmar said to have been storing explosives for mining has killed more than 45 people, according to rescue workers and independent media reports. The area, located about 3 kilometers (2 miles) south of the Chinese border, is under the control of the Ta’ang National Liberation Army, an ethnic armed group which has engaged in sporadic fighting against Myanmar’s central government. Another rescuer in Namhkam, who also spoke on condition of anonymity, said about 40 people were killed and more than 100 houses near the blast site were damaged.

item.description: A museum in eastern France on Sunday reported to police the theft of a banana that forms a core part of a multimillion-dollar artwork by Italian visual artist Maurizio Cattelan.The missing fruit -- which was taped to a wall to form the provocative work by Cattelan called "Comedian" -- was noticed by a guard on Saturday to have gone missing.

item.description: This is the first time Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy has made this accusation, which may constitute a war crime.

item.description: A concert by the US rapper Kanye West, who was supposed to perform on July 18 in Reggio Emilia in northern Italy, has been banned on public safety grounds, the authorities said.West performed in Istanbul on Saturday and is still due to appear at concerts in the Netherlands on June 6 and 8, in Tirana on July 11, and Prague on July 25.

item.description: More than 1,100 people were suspected of having contracted Ebola in the Democratic Republic of Congo and neighbouring Uganda, the head of the African Union's health agency said on Sunday in a commentary for the Financial Times.More than 1,100 suspected cases were still being investigated, he wrote in the editorial.

item.title: IAEA: Radiation levels normal at Zaporizhzhya plant after drone strike
item.description: Radiation levels at Ukraine's Russian-occupied Zaporizhzhya nuclear power plant "remain normal" following a suspected drone strike, the International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA) said on Sunday. IAEA experts stationed at the plant were able to inspect the exterior of the affected turbine hall on Sunday, according to the UN's nuclear watchdog.

item.description: At least 50 people have been killed in a blast at an explosives depot in north-eastern Myanmar, local media reported on Sunday. A further 70 people were injured in the incident in the town of Namhkam in Shan State, near the Chinese border, according to initial numbers provided by emergency services.

The ministry identified him as 26-year-old Imad Haroun Ishtayeh from Salem town, east of Nablus. It said Israeli forces shot him in a thigh in al-Ram town, and he was pronounced dead at the Palestinian Medical Complex in Ramallah.

item.title: Israeli forces make historic push inside Lebanon and complicate an Iran deal
item.description: Israeli forces are making their deepest incursion inside Lebanon since they withdrew from the country over a quarter-century ago, despite a nominal U.S.-brokered ceasefire and the first direct Israel-Lebanon talks in decades. It's a challenge in the emerging deal to extend the Iran war ceasefire as Tehran wants it to end fighting in Lebanon, too. On Sunday, Israeli forces seized a symbolic fort in southern Lebanon, Beaufort castle, perched at over 700 meters (3,000 feet) above sea level.

item.title: How Britain’s disability diagnosis epidemic reached Disneyland
item.description: It’s a sweltering morning at Disneyland Paris and the queue to collect a disabled pass is barely budging. Most of the people in the queue look and sound British: one is holding a letter from the Department of Work and Pensions (DWP); many are wearing sunflower lanyards to indicate that their disability is invisible. Behind me, I hear an English woman complaining that the whole point of getting a disability pass is not to have to queue. And yet we inch along.

item.description: Carlo Petrini, who has died aged 76 in his home town of Bra in Piedmont, was the founder of the Slow Food movement, whose mission is to create localised tradition-based alternatives to globalised fast food and the tyranny of supermarkets.

item.description: Following the deadly fire at a girls’ boarding school in Kenya, police on Sunday identified seven pupils as the arsonists using the school’s CCTV footage. No information has yet emerged regarding a possible motive.

item.title: Taiwan condemns China after New York Times reporter expelled after presidential interview
item.description: TAIPEI, May 31 (Reuters) - Taiwan's presidential office condemned China on Sunday after the New York Times said one of its reporters was expelled from the country following an interview the newspaper

item.title: Laos cave survivors help with plan to find last two missing men
item.description: So far, five men have been freed from the narrow, flooded cave but efforts are ongoing to locate two more.

item.title: Former commander of Beaufort Ridge warns against romanticizing operation, holding outpost
item.description: He warned that those who will pay the price of holding the ridge will be the soldiers and Israeli citizens living in the north. Brig.

item.title: Israel Tax Authority official questioned by Lahav 433 over alleged privacy, misconduct offenses
item.description: A few months ago, the senior official allegedly locked the door of his office while an employee was inside, confiscated the employee's mobile phone, and attempted to review recordings. A deputy director-general at the Israel Tax Authority was questioned byLahav 433 investigators on Sunday over suspicions of multiple offenses, including invasion of privacy, unlawful detention, obstruction of justice, fraud, and breach of trust.

item.title: In pictures: IDF retakes southern Lebanon's Beaufort Ridge outpost nearly three decades later
item.description: The IDF held the outpost until May 2000, when Israel withdrew its forces from southern Lebanon on the order of former prime minister Ehud Barak. IDF shares footage of operation in Beaufort Ridge, May 31, 2026.

item.title: Malta PM Abela set for new term as opposition concedes election
item.description: Malta's governing Labour Party (MLP) has declared victory in the EU country's general election. "We have made history by winning for the fourth time in a row," said Prime Minister Robert Abela in a television interview on Sunday after initial results showed his party leading the conservative Nationalist Party (PN).

item.title: Zelensky thanks Germany for Iris-T air defence system launch pad
item.description: Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky said on Sunday that Ukraine has received a new launch pad for the Iris-T air defence system from Germany. "We thank Germany for its contribution to the protection of our people," he wrote on Telegram, saying thousands of lives had been saved thanks to Berlin's "significant assistance.

item.title: Ukraine has a war lesson for NATO forces: Drone units need to be constantly on the move with command centers buried deep
item.description: Drone command centers, units, and operators are highly valuable targets, so Ukraine keeps them mobile and underground.

item.title: How the Laos cave survivors found the courage to rescue themselves
item.description: Hungry and weak, the Laos cave survivors huddled together in damp darkness for 11 days, clinging to hope as a wall of water blocked their way out.

item.title: Ukraine hits Russian energy targets and denies striking Kremlin-occupied nuclear plant
item.description: Ukraine denied Moscow's claims on Sunday that a Ukrainian drone struck the Russian-controlled Zaporizhzhia nuclear plant, as Kyiv launched fresh strikes overnight on Russian energy sites. Ukraine’s General Staff on Sunday said Ukrainian drones had struck the Saratov oil refinery in southwestern Russia, causing a “large-scale fire.” It said the extent of the damage was being clarified, and claimed the refinery has been supplying Moscow’s war effort.

item.title: Don’t let the Paras’ heroism in Northern Ireland be written out of history
item.description: On May 25, 1971, Sergeant Michael Willetts of the Parachute Regiment was killed in Springfield Road Police Station, Belfast, when a Provisional IRA suitcase bomb exploded.

item.title: Israel seizes castle in Lebanon as it expands ground offensive
item.description: The Israeli military says everyone south of the Zahrani river should leave, after it captured the strategic Beaufort Castle.

item.title: WHO chief hails 5 Ebola recoveries as a new treatment center opens in eastern Congo
item.description: Five patients have recovered from a rare type of Ebola, the head of the World Health Organization said Sunday during a visit to eastern Congo’s Bunia, a city at the heart of an outbreak. “Four people will be discharged today and there was one that was discharged the day before yesterday,” WHO Director-General Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus said during the opening of a new Ebola treatment center in Bunia, the provinical capital of Ituri. “Of course, we’re still working on vaccines and treatments but that doesn’t mean that people cannot recover from Ebola,” he added.

item.title: Petrol shortages in Crimea after Ukrainian drone attacks
item.description: Moscow - Authorities on the Crimean Peninsula - illegally annexed by Russia in 2014 - have introduced restrictions on petrol sales following Ukrainian drone attacks on oil facilities. AI-95 petrol, a premium unleaded type equivalent to 95-octane fuel sold in the European Union, will be prioritized for public and social transport, Crimea's Moscow-appointed governor Sergei Aksyonov posted on Telegram on Saturday.

item.title: IAEA issues Ukraine nuclear plant warning after Russia alleges Zaporizhzhia attack
item.description: The ZNPP sits around 30 miles from the closest portion of the front line.

item.title: O'Sullivan clan gathering in Ireland breaks world record
item.description: Almost 2,000 people with the last names O'Sullivan and Sullivan travelled from around Ireland and the world to meet in County Cork on Saturday, in the largest ever recorded gathering of people with the same surname.  The surnames O'Sullivan and Sullivan are commonplace in County Cork and southwestern Ireland.

item.title: Lebanese PM accuses Israel of 'scorched-earth policy' while defending ceasefire talks
item.description: Salam’s comments came amid the now-revealed IDF operation of crossing the Litani River, where it successfully secured the Beaufort Ridge and Wadi al-Saluki areas of southern Lebanon. Lebanese Prime Minister Nawaf Salam accused Israel of pursuing a “scorched-earth policy” as part of its operations against Hezbollah, The Guardian reported on Sunday.

item.title: Two Jerusalem residents indicted for allegedly planning terror attack against IDF soldiers
item.description: In a separate incident, police found the knife used to kill Destao Tsakol, 19, in Beersheba. A prosecutor's statement was filed against the four minors who are suspects in the case.

item.title: Michelin Used To Award Eco-Friendly Green Stars, Now, Some Restaurants Want Them Back
item.description: You may know Michelin as a tire company, or associate it with the prestigious restaurant guide and its culinary stars. But have you heard of the Green Star?

item.title: Israel captures strategic Crusader-built castle as incursion into southern Lebanon deepens
item.description: The Israeli military has captured the Crasader-era Beaufort Castle in southern Lebanon, a site it hasn’t held for 26 years, as it expands its incursion deeper into its neighbor’s territory.

item.title: Heavy rains threaten to delay search for 2 people missing in Laos cave
item.description: Heavy rains threatened to delay the search for two people missing in a flooded cave in Laos on Sunday, after the rescue of five other people who were trapped underground for over a week. Finnish diver Mikko Paasi, one of the first international rescuers to arrive at the site, told The Associated Press that rains had filled the cave up to the second chamber, preventing divers from entering the cave until pumps can lower the water level. Rescue teams from Laos and neighboring Thailand have been working together in the past week at the site in a rugged area in the central province of Xaisomboun, about 120 kilometers (75 miles) north of the capital, Vientiane.

item.title: Ballot counting starts in Malta general election
item.description: Malta was to begin counting votes Sunday in an election expected to deliver a historic fourth term for the Labour government, despite concerns over rampant overdevelopment and systemic corruption.Voting took place across the tiny Mediterranean island nation on Saturday, and ballots were ferried overnight to the Counting Hall in Naxxar, where counting begins on Sunday morning.

item.title: Israel captures southern Lebanon fortress amid expanding operations
item.description: The Israeli army said on Sunday that it had captured the strategically located Beaufort Castle in southern Lebanon and was expanding its ground operations against the Hezbollah militia in the area. A photo taken by a dpa reporter showed an Israeli flag being hoisted at the stronghold built by Crusaders in the 12th century.

item.title: Israel captures southern Lebanon fortress amid expanding operations
item.description: The Israeli army said on Sunday that it had captured the strategically located Beaufort Castle in southern Lebanon and was expanding its ground operations against the Hezbollah militia in the area. item.description: A review of the front page stories from the daily newspapers in Scotland.

item.title: Israel plants flag on medieval castle, pushes Lebanon ground operation
item.description: Israel's flag flew over the medieval fortress on Beaufort in Lebanon on Friday as it warned Lebanese civilians to evacuate a large area of the south of the country ahead of stepped up ground operations. The push to Beaufort came as the Israeli military issued a sweeping evacuation order to areas south of the Zahrani River, north of the Litani and around 40 kilometres (25 miles) from the border, warning that it was targeting the Iran-backed Hezbollah armed group.

item.title: France detains hundreds during unrest after PSG Champions League win
item.description: Hundreds of people were detained across France following Paris Saint-Germain's Champions League victory over Arsenal, as celebrations in several cities turned into unrest. Speaking at a press conference on Sunday, French Interior Minister Laurent Nuñez said 416 people had been detained nationwide, including 283 in the Paris metropolitan area.

item.title: For Exposing China’s Repression, Women Are Targeted With Deepfake AI Porn
item.description: Many female activists who take on China are targeted by deepfake porn campaigns. Some are pushing back by publicizing the images.

item.title: Ukraine renews attacks on Russian oil facilities
item.description: Ukraine dealt another blow to Russia's oil industry overnight, launching drones at a storage depot by the southern city of Taganrog on the Sea of Azov and a refinery in Saratov on the Volga River, reports said on Sunday. Russian authorities partially confirmed the strikes, which were also reported by Ukrainian media.

item.description: Four IDF soldiers were lightly wounded in the incident that killed Tyukin. Staff Sgt.

item.description: Syrian commission confirms deaths of Rania al-Abbasi’s six children, missing since 2013 under Bashar al-Assad’s rule.

item.description: In 2006 police took revenge for deadly prison riots by killing more than 500 people in alleged shootouts that others call executions. A court is to rule on compensation for victims

item.title: Inside the schools preparing Ukrainian children for war
item.description: Valeriya was only 12 years old when the Russians invaded her homeland. Four and a half years on, she says she would never kill an animal, but would not hesitate to shoot a Russian soldier.

item.title: Israeli army captures strategic castle in Lebanon in deepest incursion into country in 26 years
item.description: Israeli troops have captured a strategic mountain topped with a Crusader-built castle in southern Lebanon in the deepest incursion of the country in more than a quarter-century, the military said Sunday. The capture of Beaufort castle, near the city of Nabatiyeh, came after days of airstrikes and intense fighting in nearby villages where Israeli troops fought Hezbollah members in the rugged area. Its capture marks a major development in the latest Israel-Hezbollah war, which began on March 2 when Hezbollah fired rockets into northern Israel two days after the U.S. and Israel attacked its main backer, Iran.

item.title: IDF pushes north past Litani River, holds Beaufort Ridge outpost for first time in 26 years
item.description: The operation began several days ago, and its goal is to eliminate the threat Hezbollah poses to the citizens of northern Israel. IDF ground troops have crossed the Litani River, entering and securing the Beaufort Ridge and Wadi al-Saluki areas of southern Lebanon, the military announced on Sunday morning.

item.title: Japan defence minister denies militarism, criticises China's 'huge arsenal'
item.description: Shinjiro Koizumi makes some of Tokyo's most pointed remarks yet in the dispute over Japan's military build-up.

item.title: Heritage plates aim to celebrate post box history
item.description: Humans sent by post, occupation stories and abrupt telegrams all feature in Guernsey's postal past.

item.title: Pilot taken to hospital after air show incident
item.description: Shuttleworth says the pilot was taken to hospital after giving "a thumbs up".

item.title: Japan defence chief takes swipe at China at security meet
item.description: Japan's defence minister took a veiled swipe at China on Sunday, pledging to keep strengthening the military despite Beijing's criticism of Tokyo's increasingly muscular security stance.Japanese Defence Minister Shinjiro Koizumi hit back on Sunday, saying "nothing could be further from the truth".

item.title: Ancient shrine looks to future with upgrade plans
item.description: The charity that runs the site hopes to get the green light for improvements by early next year.

item.title: The Australian helping to return stolen English church artefacts
item.description: Items found for sale online had originally been stolen from a Hertfordshire and a Norfolk church.

item.title: How Nigeria’s ‘algorithmic apothecary’ fuels a surge in risky herbal cures
item.description: Unverified herbal remedies promoted on social media are driving rising health risks and delayed treatment in Nigeria.

item.title: Two decades later, impacts from Indonesia mud volcano linger
item.description: Harwati clasped her hands and offered a prayer on the edge of a sludge lake on the Indonesian island of Java, as dozen of residents gathered to mark two decades since mud volcano eruptions began displacing thousands.Harwati said the gathering, held on Friday to mark two decades since the disaster, was organised to remind the government that residents are still feeling the impact of the mudflow.

item.title: Iran war forces farmers to seek fertilizer alternatives from cow dung to compost
item.description: When Senegalese farmer Abou Sow first watched U.S. missiles strike Iran on social media, he had a sinking feeling it would soon affect agriculture in the West African nation. Since the war began on Feb. 28, fertilizer prices have risen by 40%. Eight years ago, he gave up chemical fertilizers for organic compost and other natural sources.

item.title: Deluded foreigners are blind to the reality of Cuba’s wretched regime
item.description: A former Cuban comandante, who had taken part in the Bay of Pigs on the Cuban side, once told me of his unease with his former communist beliefs. “If only the desire for human equality had been expressed as a spiritual quest – equality before God as it was in the Christian church – rather than as a political goal, a lot of conflict might have been averted.”

item.title: Japan defence chief takes swipe at China at security meet
item.description: Japan's defence minister took a veiled swipe at China on Sunday, pledging to keep strengthening the military despite Beijing's criticism of Tokyo's increasingly muscular security stance.Japanese Defence Minister Shinjiro Koizumi hit back on Sunday, saying "nothing could be further from the truth".

item.title: Concerns mount that Belarus could be a launchpad for a new Russian offensive in Ukraine
item.description: Over four years ago, Belarus' authoritarian President Alexander Lukashenko allowed longtime ally Russia to use his territory to invade Ukraine. Now officials in Kyiv are warning that Lukashenko could again allow his land to serve as a launchpad for more attacks by the Kremlin's forces. While Belarus has not contributed troops to battle, Lukashenko has backed President Vladimir Putin’s war effort by hosting Russia’s nuclear weapons and military infrastructure, as well as producing components for Moscow’s military industries.

item.title: Colombia's presidential election pits outgoing leader's ally against pro-Trump candidates
item.description: BOGOTÁ, Colombia (AP) — Colombians are casting ballots on Sunday in the first round of the South American nation’s presidential election, choosing between candidates with radically diverging visions for the future of peace in a country haunted by decades of armed conflict. The vote, seen as a referendum on outgoing President Gustavo Petro’s policies, comes 10 years after Colombia signed an historic peace pact with guerrillas of the Revolutionary Armed Forces of Colombia, or FARC. Criminal groups have increasingly launched drone strikes, armed attacks have plagued the race and last June, 39-year-old politician and presidential hopeful Miguel Uribe Turbay was fatally shot at a political rally.

item.title: Hundreds march in Peru against right-wing presidential candidate
item.description: Hundreds of people marched in the streets of Peruvian capital Lima on Saturday in protest against right-wing presidential candidate Keiko Fujimori, eight days before a runoff vote in the South American country.Under the banner of "Keiko Out, Fujimori Never Again," the peaceful demonstrators held signs with slogans against the candidate and her late father, former Peruvian president Alberto Fujimori, an AFP journalist observed.

item.title: Mining turns India's heat-shield hills to dust
item.description: Dizzyingly deep pits from large-scale mining scar India's ancient Aravalli mountains, threatening the future of a forested buffer that New Delhi relies on for protection from furnace-hot desert winds."If we don't protect the Aravalli, the northern Gangetic plains -- which is a food basket for the rest of the country -- would become a desert," he said.

item.title: Colombian holds presidential election marred by violence
item.description: Colombians are voting for a new president on Sunday after an election campaign marred by tension and violence. Some 41 million people are eligible to vote for a successor to left-wing President Gustavo Petro, who is constitutionally barred from running for a second term.

item.title: Soaring prices during the Iran war jeopardize travel to tourism-dependent countries in Asia
item.description: With summer around the corner, soaring prices and other complications from the war with Iran are straining the tourism-dependent economies of countries in Southeast Asia, including Thailand and Vietnam. The region's peak tourist summer season is at risk as elevated jet fuel costs coupled with ceasefire uncertainties prompt flight cancellations and higher ticket prices. Tourism in Asia has yet to fully recover from the COVID-19 pandemic.

item.title: Zelensky expecting ‘big attacks’ from Russia soon
item.description: Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky is bracing for a major attack from Russia that he said he expects to occur within the next 48 hours, citing intelligence shared by the U.S. and European allies. “Today at night, or tomorrow at night, we will have, we think that we will have big attacks from Russian side using…

item.title: 'Totally hooked': Hong Kong targets claw machine addiction
item.description: Claw machine lover Neiki Lee carefully lowers the metal jaws of a crane with a joystick into a pool of prizes, only to have the small toy slip from its clutches again and again."For a HK$70 toy, you might eventually spend 700, or even 1,700, and still not be able to grab it," Lee told AFP. "I really want to give it up.

item.title: Kanye West concert cancelled over ‘public order and safety issues’
item.description: A Kanye West concert in Italy has been cancelled as a result of “public order and safety issues”.

item.title: UN adds Israeli and Russian forces to blacklist for sexual violence during conflict
item.description: The United Nations has added Israeli and Russian security forces for the first time to its annual blacklist documenting sexual violence during conflict, including allegations of rape against detained Palestinians. The report released Friday by the U.N. lists 77 government and non-government parties in countries “credibly suspected” of “patterns of rape or other forms of…

item.title: War or peace? Colombians choose destiny in high-stakes vote
item.description: Colombians vote Sunday in a presidential election that will determine the conflict-ridden nation's response to spiraling violence, either staying left and opting for dialogue or tacking right towards all-out war.- Right-wing rivals - Dialogue with guerrillas, however, is not to the taste of Cepeda's right-wing rivals, who are betting on security fears to shunt the left out of office.

item.title: Home Front Command issues new guidelines for the North, canceling school in Lebanon border towns
item.description: The new guidelines limit gatherings in communities along the Lebanese border to no more than 50 people outdoors and 200 indoors, and beaches are closed to the public. Home Front Command updated its security guidelines for Israel's northern communities on Saturday night, in light of the increasing tensions between Hezbollah and Israel.

item.title: How stamps and postcards helped India count its people
item.description: India's post office turned stamps, postcards and letters into tools for counting a nation after independence in 1947.

item.title: UK Foreign Secretary to visit China and India amid global crises
item.description: UK Foreign Secretary Yvette Cooper will travel to China and India this week as the United Kingdom seeks to strengthen relations with both countries. Cooper will meet Chinese Vice President Han Zheng and Foreign Minister Wang Yi in Beijing on Tuesday, before visiting the southern city of Shenzhen, a major technology hub near Hong Kong.

item.title: How Putin became master of the image
item.description: From enigmatic KGB agent to wartime ruler, this is how Putin has repeatedly reinvented his image, and himself.

item.title: Actor Ian McKellen joins London march against LGBTQ+ laws abroad
item.description: British actor Ian McKellen launched a march against the criminalization of LGBTQ+ people in Commonwealth countries, branding it an "appalling situation. " The actor and activist joined protesters in central London to highlight laws in 29 Commonwealth countries where same-sex relationships remain illegal.

item.title: Nearly three years into war, Hezbollah keeps northern Israel under fire
item.description: Israeli cities remain under rocket fire in northern Israel after 965 days of conflict between Israel and Hezbollah. Israeli communities came under numerous attacks across northern Israel on May 30.

item.description: The victim, who was in her 30s, sustained slash and stab wounds in an attack earlier this week.

item.description: Police on Saturday detained over 200 people in Paris after violent clashes erupted when thousands poured onto the streets during Paris Saint-Germain's Champions League final victory.According to the French interior ministry, 326 people were detained nationwide, including 235 who were apprehended in Paris.

item.title: Caribbean Food Security, One Year After The Collapse Of USAID
item.description: The abrupt collapse of USAID one year ago impacted food security, agriculture, and climate resilience across the Caribbean.

item.title: Thousands rally in western Germany against gas-fired power stations
item.description: Thousands of climate activists gathered in Hamm, in western Germany, to protest against gas-fired power plants on Saturday, in a largely peaceful gathering, police said. The rally, held under the banner, "Future instead of gas – defend the energy transition," opposed government plans to build new gas-fired power stations and called for an expansion of renewable energy.

item.title: Three dead, one rescued after fall on Alaska’s Mount McKinley
item.description: Three climbers from a Latvian mountaineering expedition died after falling near Denali Pass on Alaska’s Mount McKinley, North America’s highest peak.

item.title: 'It's like a decaying body': Australian farmers battle mouse plague
item.description: Farmers report thousands of mice per hectare destroying crops and invading homes.

item.title: Former premier of Turks and Caicos Islands is sentenced to 4 years in landmark corruption case
item.description: A court in the Turks and Caicos Islands has sentenced former Premier Michael Misick to just over four years in prison after he was convicted in a landmark corruption case that had dragged on for years. Misick was arrested in December 2012 in Brazil, after his application for political asylum was rejected there. Former Cabinet minister McAllister Hanchell and attorney Thomas Misick — Michael Misick's brother — were also sentenced in the same case on Friday.

item.title: Are Iranian-backed militias in Iraq really ready to hand over weapons?
item.description: A Kataib Hezbollah spokesperson said he “praised what he described as a decision by ‘[armed] brothers not involved in the Islamic Resistance [of Iraq]’ to end their armed activities.
